D5 Design L3 Apply Reference

Seurat's Optical Mixing

step back from a field of blue and yellow dots and watch them fuse — into a pale grey, not the green a painter's palette would give

Pointillists placed pure dots side by side and let the eye blend them. But optical mixing averages the dots' light — closer to additive — while mixing the same pigments on a palette subtracts. Blue and yellow dots fuse to a luminous grey; blue and yellow paint makes a dull green.

Slide the viewing distance. Up close the dots stay separate; from far the eye can no longer resolve them and they merge. Compare the optical result you see against the pigment mix of the same two colours.
